mnemonics.title: RMSprop Formula
mnemonics.content: RMSprop divides learning rate by Root of Mean Square of gradients - like normalizing your step size based on how steep the path has been lately. γE[g²] + (1-γ)g² - think of E as 'Exponential averaging keeps the recent gradient history alive'.

mnemonics.title: Adam Update Order
mnemonics.content: Remember Adam's update: First compute moments (m and v), then correct bias, then update parameters. Think: 'Moments then Correct then Update' (M-C-U). Bias correction happens before using moments for parameter update.

mnemonics.title: Beta Values
mnemonics.content: β₁=0.9 (momentum - remembers 10 steps back), β₂=0.999 (squared gradient - remembers 1000 steps back). Higher β₂ because squared gradients vary more and need more smoothing.

acronyms.usage: Adaptive learning rate optimizer that divides learning rate by running average of squared gradients
Optimizer combining momentum and adaptive learning rates with bias correction
Exponential moving average of gradients, similar to velocity in physics
Exponential moving average of squared gradients, represents gradient magnitude history
Small constant (10⁻⁸) added for numerical stability to prevent division by zero
Controls how fast the moving average of squared gradients forgets past gradients, typically 0.9

visualTips.visualization: Picture a ball rolling down a valley: in steep directions (large gradients), the learning rate shrinks (smaller steps), in flat directions (small gradients), the learning rate increases (larger steps). The E[g²] acts like a 'roughness meter' for each parameter.

Imagine a heavy ball rolling down a hill: momentum carries it through small bumps (local minima), while adaptive learning rate adjusts speed based on terrain steepness. The first moment (m) is velocity, second moment (v) is terrain analysis.

Think of bias correction as 'warming up' the estimates. Initially both m and v start at 0, so they underestimate. Division by (1-βᵗ) gradually removes this underestimation as t increases, like a thermostat adjusting to room temperature.

analogies.analogy: Like a driver who adjusts speed based on road conditions: if road is rough (high gradient variance), drive slower; if smooth, drive faster. E[g²] is like the car's memory of recent road conditions.

Like a seasoned sailor: momentum (first moment) carries the ship forward even when winds are calm momentarily, while adaptive rates (second moment) adjust sail size based on how strong the winds have been historically.

analogies.anal analogy: Like a runner finishing a marathon: starts with big strides (high learning rate), gradually takes smaller steps as approaching the finish line (minimum), preventing overshooting.

analogies.analogy: Like each muscle in your body learning at different rates: arm muscles might adapt quickly while leg muscles need more practice - adaptive methods treat each parameter like individual muscles.
